thesis: Understand FQHC Behavioral Health Director role through multi-perspective council debate and synthesis
description: Council debate with Serena (Architect), Ava (Researcher), Marcus (Engineer), Rook (Risk/Compliance) to analyze director workflows, pain points, and solution requirements. Multi-round debate (Initial Positions → Responses & Challenges → Synthesis) followed by Pi's master synthesis integrating all perspectives.
verdict: Solution viable - compliance-aware dashboard addressing audit survivability

council_vote.serena_blackwood: YES - Market gap is real, architectural approach is sound
council_vote.ava_sterling: YES - Data validates demand, pricing model mitigates risk
council_vote.marcus_webb: YES - Technically feasible if we prioritize infrastructure over features
council_vote.rook_blackburn: YES - Compliance urgency creates forcing function, solution addresses real liability
council_vote.consensus: Unanimous 4-0 in favor of building this solution

key_findings: Directors are crisis managers disguised as administrators, juggling 4-6 disconnected systems
77% provider shortages, 93% burnout rate, 35-70% annual turnover in behavioral health
No platform solves both FQHC billing AND BH workflows - market gap identified
Core pain point is audit survivability, not workflow efficiency
Solution: Compliance-aware narrative dashboard with unified data layer
15-25% denial rate (vs 5-10% medical) bleeding cash - 65% never resubmitted
42 CFR Part 2 enforcement active NOW (Feb 16, 2026) - urgent compliance requirement
68-percentage-point gap between demand for denial reduction (82%) and AI adoption (14%)
Intelligent orchestration can answer 'can we take this patient?' in <10 seconds (vs 30 minutes manual)
Tiered override system (green/yellow/red/black) balances compliance and safety

key_contributions: Workforce crisis data (77% shortages, 93% burnout)
RCM complexity (15-25% denial rate, 65% never resubmitted)
42 CFR Part 2 timeline (enforcement active Feb 16, 2026)
Market gap analysis (68-point adoption gap)
Technology landscape (no platform solves FQHC + BH)
Middleware abstraction layer strategy (like Plaid for banking)
Intelligent orchestration architecture (10-second patient scheduling)
Compliance as guardrails principle (architecturally impossible to violate)
4-layer architecture (Integration → Compliance → Orchestration → Presentation)
Graceful degradation strategy (partial info > no info)
80/20 automation principle (don't try to automate every edge case)
Graceful degradation when upstream systems fail
Perceived speed = actual speed (progress indicators matter)
Integration maintenance burden analysis (permanent infrastructure work)
42 CFR Part 2 implementation complexity (database-level partitioning)
Tiered override system (green/yellow/red/black)
Compliance calendar IS product roadmap
Audit trail requirements (immutable, exportable, <60 sec report generation)
Part 2 compliance trap (FQHCs don't realize it applies if they screen for SUD)
Credentialing/authorization/documentation guardrails

layers: Orchestration Intelligence (patient scheduling + denial prevention + capacity forecasting)
Presentation UI (morning brief + compliance sidebar + crisis mode)

next_steps: Validate with 1-2 actual FQHC BH directors (if available)
